abstract: Position controllers have become the dominant interface for executing learned manipulation policies. Yet a critical design decision remains understudied: how should we choose controller gains for policy learning? The conventional wisdom is to select gains based on desired task compliance or stiffness. However, this logic breaks down when controllers are paired with state-conditioned policies: effective stiffness emerges from the interplay between learned reactions and control dynamics, not from gains alone. We argue that gain selection should instead be guided by learnability: how amenable different gain settings are to the learning algorithm in use. In this work, we systematically investigate how position controller gains affect three core components of modern robot learning pipelines: behavior cloning, reinforcement learning from scratch, and sim-to-real transfer. Through extensive experiments across multiple tasks and robot embodiments, we find that: (1) behavior cloning benefits from compliant and overdamped gain regimes, (2) reinforcement learning can succeed across all gain regimes given compatible hyperparameter tuning, and (3) sim-to-real transfer is harmed by stiff and overdamped gain regimes. These findings reveal that optimal gain selection depends not on the desired task behavior, but on the learning paradigm employed. Project website: this https URL

citations.fullCitation: M. Takegaki and S. Arimoto, “A new feedback method for dynamic control of manipulators,” ASME Journal of Dynamic Systems, Measurement, and Control, 1981.
citations.justification: This foundational paper established the global asymptotic stability of the PD control law with gravity compensation. This control law is the fundamental interface between the policy and the robot that is analyzed throughout the main paper.
citations.title: A new feedback method for dynamic control of manipulators

citations.fullCitation: J. Eßer, G. B. Margolis, O. Urbann, S. Kerner, and P. Agrawal, “Action space design in reinforcement learning for robot motor skills,” in 8th Annual Conference on Robot Learning, 2024.
citations.justification: This work is cited for framing action space selection as an inductive bias, a core perspective that the main paper explicitly extends to the selection of controller gains. This provides the key conceptual lens for the paper's investigation.
citations.title: Action space design in reinforcement learning for robot motor skills

citations.fullCitation: G. B. Margolis, M. Wang, N. Fey, and P. Agrawal, “Soft-mimic: Learning compliant whole-body control from examples,” arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.17792, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper's key observation—that a learned policy's compliance is dictated by its training incentives, not the underlying controller gains—provides the central motivation for the main paper's study. It justifies the need to analyze gains from a 'learnability' perspective rather than a 'task behavior' one.
citations.title: Soft-mimic: Learning compliant whole-body control from examples

citations.fullCitation: X. B. Peng, M. Andrychowicz, W. Zaremba, and P. Abbeel, “Sim-to-real transfer of robotic control with dynamics randomization,” in 2018 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA). IEEE, May 2018, p. 3803–3810. [Online]. Available: http://dx.doi.org/10.1109/ICRA.2018.8460528
citations.justification: This is a seminal paper on using dynamics randomization to bridge the sim-to-real gap, a standard practice that often includes randomizing PD gains. The main paper's investigation into how the nominal gain settings affect sim-to-real transfer provides crucial context and guidance for this established technique.
citations.title: Sim-to-real transfer of robotic control with dynamics randomization

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "Tune to Learn: How Controller Gains Shape Robot Policy Learning" by Bronars, Park, and Agrawal.

---

### Research Report: Tune to Learn: How Controller Gains Shape Robot Policy Learning

#### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by Antonia Bronars, Younghyo Park, and Pulkit Agrawal, all affiliated with the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). Antonia Bronars and Younghyo Park are noted as contributing equally to the work. Pulkit Agrawal is also associated with the Improbable AI Lab at MIT.

#### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Position controllers are a widely adopted low-level interface for executing learned manipulation policies in robotics due to their hardware compatibility and intuitive operation. However, unlike classical control theory, which offers established guidelines for selecting controller gains to achieve desired tracking bandwidths, disturbance rejection, or impedance characteristics, there has been a notable absence of analogous principles for robot learning contexts.

The conventional approach to gain selection often ties gains directly to desired task behavior, such as using compliant gains for contact-rich tasks or stiff gains for precision. This paper challenges this conventional wisdom by proposing that for state-conditioned learned policies, the effective stiffness or compliance emerges from the interplay between the policy's reactive outputs and the control dynamics, rather than being solely dictated by the fixed low-level gains. It recasts controller gains not as direct behavioral parameters, but as an *inductive bias* that influences *learnability* by shaping the learning problem itself.

This work builds upon and differentiates itself from several related areas:
*   **Action Spaces in Robot Learning:** Previous research has compared different action spaces (e.g., torque control versus position-controlled abstractions), often finding position control beneficial. However, these studies typically do not systematically vary gains *within* a position-controlled paradigm. This paper isolates the effect of PD gains while keeping the action representation fixed.
*   **Learning Task-Level Impedance:** Prior efforts have focused on learning variable stiffness profiles or conditioning policies on task-space stiffness. Critically, some works have observed that a policy's compliance can be determined by its training incentives rather than fixed low-level gains, providing a foundation for this study's hypothesis that gains shape the *learning process* rather than the *task-level behavior*.
*   **Sim-to-Real Transfer:** Domain randomization, a common technique for bridging the sim-to-real gap, often includes randomization of controller parameters like PD gains. Despite this, a systematic investigation into how the *nominal* gain settings, around which randomization occurs, affect transfer fidelity has been largely absent. This research contributes by exploring which gain regimes are inherently more robust for transfer.
*   **Large-Scale Robot Datasets:** An analysis of existing large-scale datasets (e.g., DROID, Open X-Embodiment) reveals a pervasive, though often undocumented, practice of using stiff controller gains, suggesting an implicit default that this paper seeks to systematically evaluate.

By systematically investigating the role of controller gains across diverse learning paradigms, this research aims to provide foundational insights and practical guidance for a critical, yet empirically driven, design decision in robot learning.

#### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to systematically investigate how position controller gains affect three core components of modern robot learning pipelines: behavior cloning (BC), reinforcement learning (RL) from scratch, and sim-to-real transfer. The authors aim to move beyond the conventional heuristic approach of selecting gains based on desired task compliance or stiffness, arguing that this logic is insufficient when controllers are paired with state-conditioned learned policies.

The central motivation stems from the hypothesis that when a robot's behavior is governed by a learned policy, the effective stiffness or compliance observed at the task level is primarily an emergent property resulting from the policy's reactive outputs interacting with the underlying control dynamics. Consequently, the low-level controller gains do not directly determine the set of achievable closed-loop behaviors. Instead, they function as an "inductive bias" or an "interface parameter" that shapes the *learning problem itself*.

Specifically, the key objectives are to answer the following questions:
*   **Decoupling:** Can a learned policy achieve stiff or compliant task-level behavior regardless of the underlying joint-level controller gains? The paper first empirically validates this claim through controlled experiments.
*   **Gain-dependent learnability:** How do different gain settings influence the ease with which a learning algorithm can fit action labels and how errors compound during closed-loop execution for behavior cloning?
*   **RL adaptability:** How do gain settings affect the training process in reinforcement learning, including the existence of successful policies, the ease of hyperparameter optimization, and sample efficiency?
*   **Sim-to-real fidelity:** How do controller gains impact the transferability of policies from simulation to the real world, particularly concerning the motor-level sim-to-real gap and potential for instability?

By addressing these questions, the authors seek to provide both conceptual clarity regarding the role of controller gains in data-driven robot learning and practical guidance for selecting optimal gain settings based on the specific learning paradigm employed.

#### 4. Methodology and Approach

The research systematically investigates the impact of position controller gains across behavior cloning, reinforcement learning, and sim-to-real transfer. A broad range of proportional (K_p) and derivative (K_d) gain setpoints, organized in a grid, were evaluated across various tasks and robot embodiments (e.g., Franka Research 3, Unitree G1).

**Decoupling Gains from Task Compliance:**
To validate the hypothesis that task-level compliance is determined by learned reactions rather than low-level gains, two counterintuitive scenarios were tested in simulation with an RL policy:
1.  **Stiff behavior with compliant gains:** A policy was trained with low-gain controllers but rewarded for maintaining a fixed pose under disturbances, using a sharp distance-based reward.
2.  **Compliant behavior with stiff gains:** A policy was trained with high-gain controllers but with a softened goal-tracking objective and a penalty for aggressive control actions (large distance tolerance and action delta penalty).
Effective Cartesian stiffness was measured via force-displacement system identification.

**A. Behavior Cloning (BC):**
*   **Challenge:** To isolate the effect of gains on learning without conflating state and action distribution shifts caused by different gain settings.
*   **Torque-to-Position Retargeting (TPR):** A two-stage procedure was developed:
    1.  Expert demonstrations were generated at high frequency (500 Hz) using torque commands.
    2.  These torque trajectories were converted into position targets for arbitrary (K_p, K_d) settings using a mathematical retargeting formula. This ensured nearly identical state trajectories while only varying the position target actions.
    3.  Retargeted commands were replayed at the policy frequency (50 Hz), and only successful rollouts were saved. TPR fidelity was validated to maintain high success rates and low joint position MSE.
*   **Training and Ablations:** BC policies (VAE generative model, MLP network, observation history length 10, action chunk size 10) were trained for each gain configuration using privileged simulation states and absolute joint-space actions. Ablations explored different network architectures, policy model classes, temporal structures, input modalities, and output representations.
*   **Gain-Dependent Teleoperation User Study:** To assess the impact on human data collection, 12 users performed a contact-rich box-pushing task with a Franka Research 3 robot using a 6-DoF SpaceMouse. For each randomly sampled gain setting, participants optimized their individual input mapping parameters to achieve the best perceived control quality, ensuring a fair comparison of teleoperation experience. Metrics included task success, completion time, and subjective control quality ratings.
*   **End-to-End Evaluation:** A real-world experiment was conducted where BC policies were trained on 100 teleoperated demonstrations collected *per-gain setting* for four corner gain configurations, and their success rates were evaluated.

**B. Reinforcement Learning (RL):**
*   **Gain-Dependent Environment Shaping:** To ensure fair comparison, key hyperparameters (e.g., action scaling, relative/absolute actions) for the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm were re-tuned for each gain setting using computational hyperparameter optimization (Optuna). This aimed to find the "best achievable performance" for each gain configuration.
*   **Tasks:** Five tasks (FR3 Joint-Reach, FR3 EE-Reach, FR3 Lift Cube, FR3 Open Drawer, Unitree G1 Track-Velocity) were used.
*   **Evaluation Metrics:**
    *   **Solution Existence:** Verified if at least one successful policy (99%+ success rate) could be discovered for each gain regime.
    *   **Hyperparameter Optimization Landscape:** Success rates across the hyperparameter space were recorded during 50 optimization trials per gain setting to assess the "ease of discovery."
    *   **Sample Efficiency:** For successful hyperparameter configurations, policies were trained with 5 random seeds to compare mean reward and standard deviation over training steps.

**C. Sim-to-Real Transfer:**
*   **System Identification (SysID):** To create gain-specific simulation environments that accurately model real-world robot dynamics, the Franka Research 3 robot was excited with sinusoidal position targets for each gain configuration. Simulation parameters (e.g., stiffness, damping, armature, friction) were then optimized using CMA-ES to minimize the spectral MSE between real and simulated joint position and velocity trajectories.
*   **Gain-Dependent Sim-to-Real Transferability:**
    *   RL policies (FR3 Joint-Reach, FR3 EE-Reach) were trained in their respective calibrated simulation environments.
    *   Hyperparameter optimization included a penalty for violating real-world robot limits (position, velocity, torque, torque rate).
    *   Zero-shot transfer was performed, deploying policies directly on the real robot without fine-tuning.
    *   **Metrics:** Trajectory error (MSE between real and simulated state trajectories) was used, along with "NN error" (RMS difference in policy outputs between sim and real observations).
*   **Ablations:** Experiments included domain randomization (perturbing sim parameters by 10%) and varying the policy control frequency (10 Hz, 20 Hz, 50 Hz, 100 Hz) to study its impact on high-frequency oscillations (jitter failures).

#### 5. Main Findings and Results

The investigation yielded distinct findings across the three robot learning paradigms, illustrating how controller gains function as an inductive bias rather than direct behavioral parameters.

**A. Behavior Cloning (BC):**
*   **Effect on Learning:** Behavior cloning consistently performed best with compliant and overdamped gains (characterized by low K_p and high K_d). Across multiple manipulation tasks (e.g., Bimanual Handover, Block Stacking), policies trained in this regime achieved significantly higher closed-loop success rates. Paradoxically, these policies often exhibited higher training and validation mean squared error (MSE) loss compared to those trained with stiff gains. This phenomenon was explained by the error-attenuation properties of compliant and overdamped controllers: lower stiffness reduces the force generated by a given action error, while higher damping dissipates perturbations more quickly, effectively limiting the resulting state deviation. This robust behavior was further demonstrated through robustness to injected action noise, where compliant/overdamped gains maintained higher success rates under identical perturbations.
*   **Effect on Teleoperation:** Contrary to potential assumptions that compliant/overdamped gains might hinder teleoperation due to sluggishness, a user study revealed comparable teleoperation efficiency and operator preference across gain regimes. When users optimized their input mapping (scaling and integration base) for each gain setting, compliant and overdamped controllers yielded similar or better success rates, completion times, and subjective ratings. This suggests that adopting these gain settings for data collection does not impose a penalty on human performance.
*   **Composed Effect (End-to-End):** When both data collection and policy learning were performed end-to-end under each gain setting on a real robot, the compliant and overdamped regime still produced the highest policy success rates. This indicates that the learning advantages of compliant gains are not offset by potential distributional differences in teleoperated data and that both effects reinforce each other.

**B. Reinforcement Learning (RL):**
*   **Solution Existence:** Reinforcement learning proved to be adaptable to a wide range of gain settings. Given appropriate environment shaping (i.e., re-tuning hyperparameters for each gain configuration), successful policies (achieving 99%+ success rate) could be discovered across all evaluated gain regimes (compliant/overdamped, compliant/underdamped, stiff/overdamped, stiff/underdamped) for various manipulation and locomotion tasks.
*   **Hyperparameter Sensitivity:** While successful policies could be found, the gain setting did modulate the hyperparameter optimization landscape. However, no single gain regime was consistently easier to optimize across all tasks, suggesting task-dependent sensitivity to hyperparameter choices.
*   **Sample Efficiency:** Once a successful hyperparameter configuration was identified, the sample efficiency and training stability of RL policies were generally comparable across different gain regimes. This indicates that the choice of gain regime does not significantly compromise the learning speed or consistency in RL.

**C. Sim-to-Real Transfer:**
*   **System Identification Quality:** System identification (SysID) achieved the lowest modeling error (MSE between simulated and real responses to sinusoidal inputs) under stiff and overdamped gain settings. This was attributed to stiff/overdamped dynamics filtering out unmodeled nonlinearities like joint flexibility and transmission dynamics, and reducing sensitivity to errors from imperfect gravity compensation.
*   **Sim-to-Real Transferability:** Despite yielding the most accurate system models, stiff and overdamped gain settings resulted in *lower* sim-to-real transferability, characterized by the largest trajectory errors and a dominant failure mode of high-frequency oscillation on the real robot. This suggests an inverse relationship between SysID accuracy and closed-loop transfer quality. The observation that policy outputs on the real robot generated with stiff/overdamped gains led to states highly unlikely in simulation, while compliant/overdamped gains yielded overlapping distributions, supports the hypothesis that stiff controllers amplify small modeling errors, causing policies to react aggressively to out-of-distribution observations.
*   **Effect of Policy Frequency:** Lowering the policy frequency (increasing the zero-order-hold duration between policy actions) significantly reduced the prevalence of high-frequency oscillation during sim-to-real transfer. For instance, jitter failures dropped from 21.8% at 100 Hz to 5.0% at 10 Hz. This finding aligns with previous work suggesting that more time between commands allows joints to settle, reducing the opportunity for policies to amplify transient errors into instability.

#### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

This research provides a systematic and comprehensive characterization of how position controller gains influence various aspects of robot policy learning, offering both conceptual clarity and practical guidelines for a historically underexplored design decision.

**Conceptual Impact:**
The study fundamentally reframes the role of controller gains in data-driven robot learning. Rather than viewing gains as static parameters that directly determine task-level behavior (e.g., stiffness or compliance), the authors demonstrate that gains primarily act as an "inductive bias" or an "interface" that shapes the *learnability* of policies. This perspective highlights that effective task behaviors emerge from the learned policy's reactive dynamics interacting with the underlying controller, rather than solely from the controller's fixed properties.

**Practical Implications and Guidance:**
The findings offer concrete recommendations tailored to specific robot learning paradigms:
*   **Behavior Cloning:** For tasks where policies are learned from demonstrations, compliant and overdamped gain regimes are demonstrably superior. This is a critical insight, as it challenges the prevalent practice of using stiff controllers for data collection, a norm potentially rooted in historical control practices rather than empirical validation for learning. The finding that compliant gains do not compromise teleoperation efficiency further strengthens this recommendation for practitioners.
*   **Reinforcement Learning:** While RL exhibits robustness and can converge to successful policies across a broad spectrum of gain settings, the specific gain configuration influences the hyperparameter optimization landscape. This suggests that while RL is versatile, careful tuning of training parameters remains essential to achieve optimal performance, regardless of the gain choice.
*   **Sim-to-Real Transfer:** The most counterintuitive finding pertains to sim-to-real transfer: stiff and overdamped gains, despite leading to the lowest system identification errors, paradoxically *reduce* transferability and exacerbate high-frequency oscillations. This indicates that traditional metrics for simulation fidelity (e.g., low modeling error) do not directly correlate with successful closed-loop policy transfer. For robust sim-to-real deployment, researchers should consider more compliant and less overdamped gain settings, and leverage lower policy control frequencies as a mitigation strategy against unmodeled dynamics.

**Future Research Directions:**
The framework and findings presented open several avenues for future work:
*   **Generalization to Complex Embodiments:** Investigating whether these gain-dependent trends extend to modern humanoid robots that increasingly use RL-trained whole-body tracking policies as low-level controllers.
*   **Learning from Implicit Actions:** Exploring the implications for learning paradigms that infer action labels from observed next-timestep states (e.g., human videos, wearable devices), which implicitly assume perfect target tracking. The results suggest this assumption might be suboptimal for imitation learning and warrant re-evaluation.
*   **Targeted Domain Randomization:** Leveraging these insights to design more targeted domain randomization strategies for sim-to-real, potentially prioritizing randomization around gain regimes that are robust to unmodeled dynamics, rather than treating all configurations as equally viable.

In summary, this research provides foundational evidence that controller gain selection is a crucial, learning-paradigm-dependent design decision with significant impact on policy performance and transferability, moving the field towards more principled and empirically grounded practices in robot learning.
overview:
## The Role of Controller Gains in Modern Robot Learning

Position-controlled robots rely on a foundational control loop to track commanded trajectories. In this paradigm, a high-level policy outputs a target joint configuration $q_d$, and a low-level proportional-derivative (PD) controller generates the actual joint torques $\tau$ required to reach that target. This relationship is typically governed by the equation:

$$\tau = K_p(q_d - q) - K_d\dot{q} + \tau_{ff}$$

where $q$ and $\dot{q}$ represent the current joint position and velocity, $\tau_{ff}$ is a feed-forward term (often for gravity compensation), and the proportional gain $K_p$ and derivative gain $K_d$ are parameters that determine the controller's stiffness and damping, respectively.

![Overview of the study: tuning controller gains for learnability rather than task-level behavior.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02523v1/main-figure-gain-setting-2.png)
*Caption: Position controller gains ($K_p$ and $K_d$) are often selected based on desired task compliance, but this research suggests they should instead be optimized to improve policy learnability.*

Historically, roboticists have selected these gains using heuristics based on the task at hand. For tasks requiring precision, such as high-speed assembly, "stiff" gains (high $K_p$) are used to ensure the robot tracks its targets as closely as possible. For contact-rich manipulation, such as opening a door or cleaning a surface, "compliant" gains (low $K_p$) are preferred to allow the robot to yield under external forces, preventing damage.

However, modern robot learning shifts the burden of defining behavior from the controller to a state-conditioned policy. When a policy is reactive—meaning it updates its commands based on the robot's current state—the effective behavior of the system is no longer determined by the gains alone. Instead, it emerges from the interaction between the policy's response and the control dynamics. This work investigates how these gains function as an *inductive bias* that shapes the learnability, training stability, and transferability of learned policies.

## Decoupling Low-Level Gains from High-Level Behavior

A central claim of this research is that the effective stiffness of a robot is decoupled from its low-level controller gains when it is governed by a reactive policy. In classical control, if a robot has low $K_p$ gains, it will necessarily be "soft" or compliant to external disturbances. This research demonstrates that a learned policy can override this default behavior.

By training a reinforcement learning (RL) policy with compliant gains to maintain a rigid pose despite external forces, the authors show that the policy learns to compensate for its own compliance by outputting aggressive, counteractive target positions. Conversely, a policy trained with stiff gains can be incentivized to act compliantly by learning to move its targets in the direction of an external force.

![Quantifying the emergence of task-level stiffness across different gain regimes.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02523v1/existence_proof_quant.png)
*Caption: Effective Cartesian stiffness is not strictly tied to low-level joint gains. Policies can learn to be stiff even with compliant controller gains (orange line) or compliant with stiff gains (blue line).*

The effective stiffness $K_{eff}$ is measured by the relationship between a displacement $\Delta x$ and the resulting force $\Delta F$:

$$K_{eff} = -\frac{\Delta F}{\Delta x} \text{ [N/m]}$$

The results confirm that $K_{eff}$ is an emergent property. This finding is significant because it suggests that we do not need to choose gains to "match" the task's mechanical requirements. Instead, we are free to choose gains that make the policy easier to learn or more robust to real-world errors.

## Behavior Cloning: Why Compliant Gains Improve Learnability

Behavior cloning (BC) is the process of learning a policy by imitating an expert's actions. A major challenge in BC is the compounding of errors: small mistakes by the policy lead the robot to states it never saw during training, causing even larger subsequent mistakes.

The authors investigated how gains affect this error propagation. To do this fairly, they developed a technique called **Torque-to-Position Retargeting (TPR)**. This allows them to take a single set of expert torque demonstrations and convert them into target position actions for any arbitrary $(K_p, K_d)$ gain setting. This ensures that the only difference between training scenarios is the gain setting, while the physical motion remains identical.

![A robot performing a dish unloading task, one of the contact-rich manipulation environments used to evaluate behavior cloning.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02523v1/dishrack-unload.png)
*Caption: Manipulation tasks like unloading a dishrack provide a complex environment for evaluating how controller gains influence imitation learning performance.*

The experiments revealed a consistent trend: **compliant and overdamped gains (low $K_p$, high $K_d$) lead to significantly higher success rates in BC.** 

This might seem counterintuitive because stiff gains result in lower training error (the robot follows the expert's path more accurately in simulation). However, the compliant/overdamped regime is more "forgiving." If the policy outputs a slightly incorrect target $q_d$, a compliant controller generates less torque, resulting in a smaller physical deviation. Simultaneously, high damping $K_d$ prevents the robot from oscillating after a perturbation. This "error attenuation" property prevents small policy errors from spiraling into catastrophic task failures.

## Teleoperation and the Human Element

If compliant gains are better for learning, does using them make it harder for humans to provide demonstrations? High-gain, stiff robots feel responsive, while low-gain robots can feel "mushy" or laggy to a human operator.

To address this, the authors conducted a user study where participants teleoperated a robot to push a box into a target zone. For each gain setting, the users were allowed to adjust an **input mapper** $\phi(u, x) \to x_{des}$, which translates their raw control inputs $u$ (from a 3D mouse) into desired target positions $x_{des}$.

![Users adjusting input mapping parameters during the teleoperation study.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02523v1/output3.png)
*Caption: The teleoperation setup allowed users to customize how their inputs mapped to robot motion, ensuring that comparisons between gain settings were fair to the human operator.*

The study found that when users were given the opportunity to tune their individual input mapping parameters, they performed just as well with compliant gains as they did with stiff gains. In fact, many users found the compliant settings easier to handle in contact-rich tasks because the robot was less likely to jerk or stall when hitting the box. This implies that robot learning practitioners can safely adopt compliant gain regimes for data collection without sacrificing the quality or speed of human demonstrations.

## Reinforcement Learning: Adaptability and Optimization Landscapes

Unlike behavior cloning, reinforcement learning (RL) allows the robot to explore and learn from its own mistakes. The authors tested whether the same gain-dependent trends held for RL across tasks ranging from cube lifting to bimanual handovers.

![System identification results showing the fit between real robot data and simulated joint trajectories.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02523v1/sysid_final_with_markers.png)
*Caption: System identification ensures that the simulation environments used for reinforcement learning accurately reflect the specific dynamics of each gain configuration.*

The key finding for RL is its high degree of **adaptability**. Successful policies could be discovered for nearly every gain setting, provided that the training hyperparameters (like action scaling) were re-tuned for each specific configuration. While the "ease of discovery" (how many hyperparameter settings lead to success) varied depending on the task and gain, no single gain regime was a universal winner.

This suggests that for RL-based workflows, the specific choice of gain is less critical for the final performance of the policy, as the RL algorithm naturally adapts its reactive behavior to the underlying hardware dynamics. However, the choice of gains still influences how sensitive the training process is to hyperparameter selection.

## The Sim-to-Real Paradox: Accuracy vs. Robustness

One of the most surprising findings of the research involves the transition from simulation to the real world. To prepare for this transfer, researchers perform **System Identification (SysID)** to make the simulation as accurate as possible.

The authors found that stiff gain settings yielded the most accurate system models. Because stiff controllers dominate the robot's unmodeled nonlinearities (like joint friction or slight arm flex), the simulated robot was a very close match to the real one.

![Real-world joint tracking showing high-frequency jitter when using stiff controller gains.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02523v1/joint_7_achieved_only.png)
*Caption: Stiff gain settings are prone to high-frequency oscillations and jitter during zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, despite appearing highly accurate in offline modeling.*

However, this accuracy did not translate to successful transfer. When policies were deployed on the real robot, **stiff gain settings often failed due to high-frequency oscillations (jitter).** This happens because stiff controllers are highly sensitive to small discrepancies between sim and real observations. A tiny modeling error can cause the policy to react aggressively, which the stiff controller immediately translates into a large torque, leading to instability.

In contrast, compliant and overdamped gains proved much more robust. They inherently "smooth out" the policy's reactions to modeling gaps. Furthermore, the authors found that lowering the policy's update frequency (e.g., running the policy at $10$ Hz or $20$ Hz instead of $100$ Hz) was an effective way to mitigate jitter, as it gives the robot's physical joints more time to settle between discrete commands.

## Practical Recommendations for Robot Learning

This systematic investigation provides a set of data-driven guidelines for researchers and engineers in robot learning:

1.  **For Behavior Cloning:** Use compliant and overdamped gains (low $K_p$, high $K_d$). This regime attenuates the compounding errors that often degrade imitation learning performance.
2.  **For Data Collection:** Do not be afraid to use compliant gains for human teleoperation. Provided the input mapping is tuned, humans are highly adaptable, and the resulting data will be better suited for learning.
3.  **For Reinforcement Learning:** RL is versatile, but ensure you re-tune your action scales and other environment hyperparameters whenever you change controller gains.
4.  **For Sim-to-Real:** Do not rely solely on modeling accuracy as a metric for transferability. Stiff gains might look better in simulation but often fail on hardware due to unmodeled dynamics. Consider compliant/overdamped gains and lower policy frequencies to achieve stable zero-shot transfer.

By viewing controller gains as an inductive bias rather than a fixed mechanical property, this work provides a framework for designing robot control interfaces that are optimized for learning rather than just traditional tracking.

keyInsights: Controller gains act as an 'inductive bias' that shapes the learnability of policies, rather than directly determining task-level behaviors like stiffness or compliance, which emerge from the policy's reactive outputs.
For behavior cloning, compliant and overdamped gain settings lead to more robust policies due to error-attenuation properties, despite potentially higher training loss.

Traditional metrics for simulation fidelity (e.g., low system identification error with stiff gains) do not directly correlate with successful closed-loop policy transfer; stiff gains can amplify modeling errors into instability in real-world deployment.
originalProblem: Absence of systematic principles for selecting position controller gains in robot learning, unlike established guidelines in classical control theory.
Conventional approaches tie low-level gains directly to desired task behavior (e.g., compliance for contact), which may be an insufficient heuristic for state-conditioned learned policies.
Lack of understanding regarding how controller gains impact policy learnability, hyperparameter optimization for RL, and the fidelity of sim-to-real transfer.
results: Behavior cloning policies achieved significantly higher closed-loop success rates with compliant and overdamped gains (low K_p, high K_d), with comparable teleoperation efficiency for data collection.

Reinforcement learning demonstrated adaptability, with successful policies (99%+ success rate) discoverable across all evaluated gain regimes given appropriate environment-specific hyperparameter tuning.

Stiff and overdamped gains, despite yielding the lowest system identification errors, resulted in lower sim-to-real transferability and exacerbated high-frequency oscillations on real robots, while lower policy frequencies mitigated these failures.
solution: A systematic evaluation of proportional (K_p) and derivative (K_d) gain setpoints was conducted across behavior cloning (BC), reinforcement learning (RL), and sim-to-real transfer scenarios.
For BC, a Torque-to-Position Retargeting (TPR) method was developed to isolate the effect of gains on action targets while maintaining nearly identical state trajectories.
For sim-to-real transfer, gain-specific simulation environments were created through system identification, and RL policies were trained and evaluated for zero-shot deployment.
summary.summary: Researchers at MIT systematically analyzed how position controller gains influence robot policy learning across behavior cloning, reinforcement learning, and sim-to-real transfer. The work found that compliant and overdamped gains enhance behavior cloning and teleoperation efficiency, while stiff gains, despite yielding better system models, paradoxically reduce sim-to-real transferability.

title: Tune to Learn: How Controller Gains Shape Robot Policy Learning
